text: The surgeon demonstrates the preferred port position for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, placing the camera port in a supraumbilical location.

The surgeon creates an epigastric port two centimeters below the lower border of the liver, ensuring it is directed towards the gallbladder. The assisting ports are positioned in the mid-clavicular and mid-axillary lines, maintaining triangulation with the camera port.

The surgeon uses an instrument to push and locate the gallbladder, assessing adhesions and identifying the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD) junction. The midclavicular line port is being created, ensuring a distance of ten to twelve centimeters to the target organ, with similar spacing for the anterior axillary line port.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of the epigastric port below the liver, ensuring ergonomic positioning for instrument triangulation during gallbladder manipulation. Both instruments are positioned to facilitate stress-free procedure execution.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the gallbladder, carefully separating flimsy adhesions from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon demonstrates the proximity of the duodenum to the gallbladder, highlighting the importance of assistant-provided traction to safely mobilize the gallbladder away from the duodenum during the procedure.

The surgeon has divided all peritoneal attachments, safely pushing the duodenum away. The Ruvius sulcus is visible in the background, indicating that further mobilization of the peritoneum is unnecessary as the common bile duct (CBD) is not the area of interest.
The surgeon begins scoring the peritoneum close to the gallbladder, marking the critical starting point for further dissection.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum close to the gallbladder in a thin patient, with visible green and red markings indicating the course of the common bile duct (CBD) and hepatic artery. The dissection is performed carefully, maintaining a safe distance from these structures.
The surgeon begins scoring the anterior peritoneum, which is directly visible in the surgical field, preferring this approach over the posterior peritoneum.
The surgeon performs a key step in the cholecystectomy, working medial to the cystic artery, which is becoming visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum medial to the cystic artery, removing fatty tissue between the artery and duct. The procedure progresses towards the posterior side, where the peritoneum is scored to connect spaces on both sides.
The surgeon applies a short burst of cautery to the peritoneum, allowing carbon dioxide to enter and visibly open up the avascular plane for safe dissection.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to coagulate small vessels, ensuring precise control over tissue coagulation before proceeding with cutting or desiccation modes for further dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the lower end of the gallbladder from the cystic plate, ensuring a comprehensive critical view of safety. The dissection at the liver bed level is clearly visible.
The surgeon addresses the final attachments near the base of the cystic plate at the lower end of the gallbladder, using a hook to coagulate any small oozing vessels or venules present.

The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery instrument to coagulate bleeding at the base of the liver bed during the gallbladder dissection, ensuring secure hemostasis without causing additional tissue damage.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to enter the avascular plane, characterized by minimal fibrosis, to continue the dissection of the lower third of the gallbladder. This area is carefully dissected to ensure a clear critical view of safety.

The surgeon works on the anterior side, dissecting medial to the vessel to elongate the cystic artery. This step provides a good length of the artery for coagulation, ensuring secure hemostasis and preventing retraction or clip loosening.

The surgeon increases traction on the gallbladder, using a monopolar hook to remove fatty tissue near the gallbladder. A window is created to the posterior side, allowing visibility of the liver through the newly formed opening.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to divide fatty tissue near the gallbladder, widening the window created on the posterior side. Fatty tissue near the vessel is also being removed, and the hook is used to address fat holding the lower end of the gallbladder on the anterior side.
The surgeon scores the peritoneum, revealing an avascular plane for dissection. The posterior dissection is initiated, utilizing the clear view to visualize two structures leading to the gallbladder.
The surgeon continues to release the peritoneum to improve the critical view of safety, revealing the extent of dissection performed on the liver bed and the lower end of the gallbladder.

The surgeon releases small fatty attachments and dissects the peritoneum on the anterior side, ensuring the porta hepatis is clear from the dissection area. The gallbladder remains attached to the cystic plate.
The surgeon uses an instrument to milk the duct, ensuring any small stones are pushed back into the gallbladder. Two structures are clearly visible, indicating the gallbladder attachment.

The surgeon uses a large atraumatic instrument to apply the milking technique on the duct at the lower end of the gallbladder. The assisting hand holds the duct to prevent movement. A medial enlarge clip is applied to ligate the cystic duct, ensuring hemostasis even if the cystic artery is cut during traction.

The surgeon assesses the width of the cystic duct and applies a ligature using a vial suture to ensure complete obliteration, as the clip was insufficient. The suture technique is visible, with options for using different suture materials like PDS.
The surgeon ties knots to secure the cystic duct, aiming to prevent bile spillage into the surgical area, which could cause postoperative ileus and infection.

The surgeon divides the duct, causing it to fall laterally and downward, indicating it is not the common bile duct (CBD). The gallbladder is then released from the cystic plate with traction, revealing the correct dissection planes and small flimsy attachments, which are carefully separated without penetrating the liver capsule.
The surgeon adjusts traction around the surgical field to stretch the tissues, facilitating the identification of a clear avascular plane, minimizing oozing and ensuring effective hemostasis.

The surgeon changes traction to improve the view and dynamics for dissection, using a monopolar hook to separate flimsy attachments. The tip of the hook is kept clear to minimize tissue adherence and reduce smoke generation, ensuring unobstructed vision.
The surgeon uses the belly of a monopolar hook to bluntly dissect small vessels and branches, avoiding the tip to minimize trauma and prevent accidental entry into the liver bed.

The surgeon changes traction and counter traction to better identify dissection planes, focusing on releasing the remaining posterior portion of the gallbladder. The anterior side is visible, allowing continued release while monitoring the liver bed for any bleeding. Small fibrous attachments are visible during this process.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect small vessels running through the peritoneum from the liver parenchyma, coagulating them to secure hemostasis. The liver bed is visible with a small blood clot that has been coagulated. The gallbladder is manipulated carefully to avoid complications, ensuring a clean dissection without the need for drains.

The surgeon places the gallbladder into an endo bag, holding it at the area of ligation. A small stab incision is made, and a suction device is inserted into the gallbladder to remove all bile, causing it to collapse. Despite a large stone, it is extracted through the defect. The endo bag is carefully removed to prevent tearing.

The surgeon closes the tenamen ports using a port suture needle to prevent spillage of stones and bile, which could lead to infection. A laparoscopic-guided TAP block is administered, visibly causing the transversus abdominis (TA) muscle to bulge.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic duct and artery from the anterior perspective. Small peritoneal attachments are being dissected, ensuring only two structures are identified. The gallbladder is positioned higher, while the porta hepatis is lower, with the view confirmed from the posterior side.

The surgeon captures a doublet view showing both the posterior and anterior perspectives, confirming the presence of one duct and one artery. The Ruvius sulcus is prominently visible, with the common bile duct (CBD) located below it at the portal level.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum close to the gallbladder, initially making a mistake by dissecting near the cystic lymph node (Lymph node of Lund). The bulging lymph node is visible above the surgical hook, serving as a guide to adjust the dissection line. The surgeon corrects the dissection to stay above the lymph node, ensuring the ligation of the artery is also above this landmark.

The surgeon continues the dissection in the correct plane, revealing a clear cleavage plane between the artery and the gallbladder. The Ruvius sulcus is visible, aiding in maintaining a safe dissection path. A tubular structure is also visible before the dissection begins.
The surgeon releases small attachments over the gallbladder, dissecting between the visible fat and ducts.

The surgeon identifies the Ruvius sulcus on the left side of the screen, with the dissection occurring below it. A tubular structure is visible, likely the cystic duct, with the gallbladder and cystic duct junction positioned higher. A blue structure is observed inside the duct.

The surgeon retracts the hepatic artery with the right instrument, exposing the portal vein and the common bile duct (CBD) on the left side. The dissection reveals the bare portal vein, prompting caution. The surgeon releases fibrotic attachments near the Ruvius sulcus, exposing the proximal portion of the hepatic duct and the CBD area.
The surgeon identifies the proximal course of the hepatic duct and the window between the short, wide cystic duct and the hepatic duct. The abnormal anatomy is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon meticulously dissects around the Mohenian hump, revealing the hepatic artery running below the instrument and the hepatic duct positioned beneath it. Small fibrous strands are visible, which are coagulated under controlled conditions.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the artery, cystic duct, and hepatic duct. The abnormal and prominent vessel is noted in this case of cholecystitis. Gauze is introduced to improve visibility amidst surrounding fat and inflamed tissues. Using a monopolar hook, the surgeon identifies the border of the gallbladder, with small oozing observed in the area.

The surgeon begins to visualize the gallbladder amidst surrounding fat and small vessels, which are oozing. The gallbladder is hugged and the peritoneum and fat are released to clearly identify the gallbladder for precise dissection. A tubular structure becomes visible as the peritoneum is incised anteriorly towards the lower end of the gallbladder, approaching its attachment to the cystic plate.

The surgeon uses gauze to manage the inflammatory fluid surrounding the gallbladder, which is visibly elongated in this patient. Despite challenges, the surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to safely dissect the gallbladder laparoscopically, separating inflammatory attachments amidst the abundant fluid.

The surgeon creates a window around the cystic duct, gently dissecting small attachments strand by strand. The liver is visible on the opposite side. The artery is exposed, and a window is opened around it as well. The elongated gallbladder is held midway, revealing a single wide duct entering it, which is ligated using intracorporeal sutures.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to demonstrate the course of the common bile duct (CBD) and ligates the duct proximally, leaving a small cuff of tissue to prevent ligature slippage. Sludge is visible in the area being cut, prompting the surgeon to milk the duct to push any remnants towards the gallbladder. A combination of sharp and blunt dissection is employed to clearly identify tissue planes. The gallbladder is removed, and hemostasis is secured using a bipolar instrument.

The surgeon encounters fibrotic adhesions and thick fat due to delayed presentation, requiring precise sharp dissection to clearly identify the gallbladder along its entire course. Progress is made as the adhesions are released.

The surgeon releases fatty attachments around the gallbladder, starting from the posterior area and moving to the anterior segment. The traction is adjusted by repositioning the toothed instrument from the fundus to midway along the gallbladder due to its inferior extension, ensuring adequate traction for dissection.

The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar instrument to perform a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, coagulating small vessels to minimize thermal spread. A window is opened, revealing two structures entering the gallbladder: the artery and the cystic duct. The surgeon releases the lower end of the gallbladder from the cystic plate to establish the critical view of safety (CVS). The artery and cystic duct are clearly visible, with the cystic duct appearing long. The common bile duct (CBD) is also visible at the lower level. The gallbladder is densely adherent to the liver bed, and the surgeon ligates the vessels to ensure a safe cholecystectomy.

The surgeon employs a fundus-first technique to improve the dissection plane, connecting the dissection from the medial side with the fundus-first approach. The remaining few attachments are densely adherent, presenting a challenge due to the patient's previous laparotomy and acute cholecystitis.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis after starting the port from Palmer's point, removing small bowel adhesions to access the gallbladder. The gallbladder appears thick-walled and distended. Using an obturator, the surgeon punctures the gallbladder to suction out inflammatory fluid. A toothed forceps is used to manage the area, revealing significant adhesions and inflammation. The ducts are identified and isolated, with surrounding fat released to encircle the duct.

The surgeon uses a landmark to continue dissection, encountering a pulsatile bleed from the partially coagulated cystic artery. Gauze is applied to apply pressure and suction is used to clear the blood, preventing obstruction of the surgical view. The bleeding stops, and the surgeon proceeds to release the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon encounters thick peritoneal attachments, which are unusually dense in this patient. An accidental entry into the gallbladder reveals a visible stone. The gallbladder wall becomes clearer as the surgeon holds it and performs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection. The gallbladder peels off easily when the correct plane is found, due to severe inflammation and infection.

The surgeon observes the irregular surface of the cystic plate, emphasizing the need for caution to avoid entering the liver parenchyma. Due to difficulty in identifying the gallbladder wall, the decision is made to leave the posterior wall attached to the liver while removing other adhesions.

The surgeon performs blunt atraumatic dissection using a suction apparatus to separate inflammatory adhesions, which peel off easily due to early intervention. Bile extravasation is visible among the adhesions.

The surgeon identifies the gallbladder amidst severe inflammation, with the omentum adhered to it. A large vessel is used as a landmark, and dissection is performed above and lateral to it to avoid damage. A window is created to access the gallbladder, and the wide cystic duct is ligated. An intraoperative cholangiogram is performed to check for any remaining stones.

The surgeon has ligated the cystic duct and inserted a small infant catheter through it, administering a wash and injecting dye. Using a C-arm, the dye flow is observed, clearly visualizing the intrahepatic ducts and the common bile duct (CBD), with spillage into the duodenum. The cystic duct junction is divided, and an endo loop is applied to both the proximal and distal areas. A large stone is crushed inside an endo bag to facilitate removal, avoiding the need to enlarge the port incision. Dense fibrotic attachments are visible, causing difficulty in handling the gallbladder.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on a thickened gallbladder with visible pus due to acute cholecystitis. Despite the presence of macronodular cirrhosis, the adhesions are not dense, allowing for easy separation using atraumatic dissection and suction. The gallbladder is identified more clearly, and a puncture needle is used to aspirate its contents.

The surgeon is unable to demonstrate a critical view of safety and opts to leave a cuff of tissue at the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct. Using a monopolar hook, the gallbladder is incised at this level, revealing sloughed-off tissue inside due to infection. A plane is created between the gallbladder and cystic plate to carefully release adhesions.

The surgeon releases the last attachments of the gallbladder, disconnecting it completely to perform a safe cholecystectomy. The sloughed-off tissue inside the gallbladder is visible, ensuring no risk of bile duct injury.

The surgeon disconnects the gallbladder up to the fundus, leaving it partially attached for traction. The infected and sloughed material is visible in the liver bed. Using bipolar coagulation, the surgeon prepares the healthy mucosa for suturing. With no available planes, the tissue is trimmed further, and three sutures are placed on the remnant segment, which appears larger due to laparoscopic magnification.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to aspirate the gallbladder, causing it to collapse visibly. The thickened gallbladder wall is then held and manipulated. The clip transitions to show the area of the common bile duct (CBD) being explored, a procedure typically performed in patients with failed ERCP.

The surgeon releases adhesions starting from the cystic duct, creating a clear critical view of safety (CVS) in the area. The dissection progresses inferiorly towards the duodenum, gradually revealing the common bile duct (CBD).

The surgeon meticulously dissects inflammatory adhesions using cautery, sharp, and blunt techniques, revealing the duodenum lateral to the instrument. The bile duct appears distended and wide due to obstructing stones. The gallbladder is exposed up to the duodenum level, and a trans-cystic flushing attempt is made, with a balloon visible moving into the dilated duct. A scope is inserted to check for stones, leading to a small choledochotomy when no major stones are visualized.

The surgeon performs a choledochotomy, resulting in profuse bleeding, which is controlled using an air dramatic. Sharp dissection is employed to avoid electrocautery, and a balloon is inserted to flush the bile duct. Sludge is visibly expelled, with no major stones observed. The balloon is inflated and retrieved, releasing additional sludge material.
The surgeon places a small infant feeding tube as a stent within the choledochotomy site and sutures the choledochotomy over the stent in a continuous manner, ensuring the duct remains open.

The surgeon performs a choledochoduodenostomy by making a linear choledochotomy along the length of the common bile duct (CBD) and a corresponding incision on the duodenum. The first sutures are placed at the nine o'clock and six o'clock positions on the duodenum using a monofilament PDS suture, ensuring a wide open anastomosis. The posterior bite is completed, and the anterior wall is sutured precisely, avoiding inclusion of the posterior wall, resulting in a completed anastomosis.

The duodenum is observed moving smoothly near the surgical site. The gallbladder remains attached to maintain control while identifying the choledochal cyst and common bile duct (CBD) area. An endo loop is applied at the junction of the gallbladder and CBD with clear visibility.
The surgeon addresses bleeding from the liver and inflammatory area by initially applying gauze, which fails to stop the oozing. An abgel is then placed in the area, effectively halting the bleeding.

The surgeon attempts a blunt dissection in an area with dense fibrotic adhesions, inadvertently injuring the duodenum. The duodenal mucosa is visibly open, with bile leaking from the duodenum. A small balloon is inserted through the trans-cystic area, emerging through the iatrogenic injury in the duodenum. The surgical field is washed, and fluid is seen exiting through the duodenum.

The surgeon inserts a small double J stent into the common bile duct (CBD) and applies a clip over it. The opening is sutured using a PDS suture, with the surgeon taking multiple bites. A small pexy with the omentum is performed. The adhesions are fibrotic, and the gallbladder appears as a small thimble-like structure after dissection.

The surgeon dissects the remnant gallbladder, revealing its small size and its adherence to the common bile duct (CBD). A small rent in the CBD is visible with bile flowing. The gallbladder is released from adhesions, leaving a portion near the liver for manipulation. The surgeon sutures the rent in the CBD with interrupted sutures, ensuring no bile leak by checking with a dry gauze. The posterior wall of the gallbladder remains intact as the rest is removed.
